https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
JACKSON — A recent fentanyl strike force operation in Lee and Prentiss counties resulted in multiple criminal charges and six immigration detainers, Mississippi Attorney General Lynn Fitch announced.

The joint operation involved the Mississippi Attorney General’s Office, the North Mississippi Narcotics Task Force, Homeland Security Investigations, U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement and the Mississippi Alcoholic Beverage Control.

Authorities said the operation resulted in two felony charges, six misdemeanor charges and six ICE detainers.

Fitch said the operation marks the first enforcement action completed by the Mississippi Attorney General’s Office under the federal 287(g) Task Force program, which allows certain state and local law enforcement agencies to partner with ICE on immigration enforcement.

“Our office is proud to participate in the 287(g) program to make Mississippi safer than ever before,” Fitch said in a statement. “We are grateful to work alongside such dedicated and skilled federal, state and local law enforcement partners. Together, we are taking dangerous criminals off the streets and restoring peace and security in our communities.”

Deputy Field Office Director Brian Acuna of the ICE New Orleans Field Office said the program is intended to enhance public safety by targeting criminal activity linked to illegal immigration.

“This is exactly what the ICE 287(g) program is all about — keeping the public safe from the threat of fentanyl and removing criminal illegal aliens from our communities,” Acuna said.

Under Section 287(g) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, state and local law enforcement agencies can receive federal training and authority to help identify individuals who may be subject to immigration enforcement while in local custody.

According to the Attorney General’s Office, the agency was the first in Mississippi to participate in the program by training and certifying all of its sworn law enforcement officers as members of the 287(g) Task Force.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON — A recent fentanyl strike force operation in Lee and Prentiss counties resulted in multiple criminal charges and six immigration detainers, Mississippi Attorney General Lynn Fitch announced.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Fentanyl Strike Force Operation in North Mississippi Results in ICE Detainers, Charges - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
PEARL — A GoFundMe fundraiser has been launched to help the owner of The Edible Paper Store & Co. in Pearl recover after a fire damaged the specialty baking supply shop and forced it to close indefinitely.

The store, located at 2901 Old Brandon Road, specializes in edible images, custom cake toppers and decorations for cakes, cupcakes and other baked goods. Owner Kat Reynolds said the fire damaged the building’s electrical system and wiring, requiring extensive repairs before the business can reopen.

According to a description on the fundraiser page, Reynolds runs much of the business herself, from designing custom edible images and toppers to managing daily operations. The fire destroyed or damaged several essential items she relies on to produce her products, prompting supporters to organize the online fundraiser to help replace equipment and supplies.

“Rebuilding is crucial not only for her business but also for the community,” the GoFundMe description states. “Every donation, big or small, helps her recover and continue to provide the sweet treats that bring joy to so many.”

Reynolds said in a social media update that while the storefront will remain closed during repairs, she plans to continue limited operations from home so customers can still order edible image printing and cake toppers.

“It could have been much worse,” Reynolds wrote, noting that most of the store’s inventory remained intact despite the fire.

Customers needing supplies are encouraged to contact the store by email or phone, and staff members will retrieve items from the shop once or twice daily while cleanup and repairs continue.

The business is also searching for a potential new location in the Pearl area while the current building undergoes repairs.

The Edible Paper Store & Co. is typically open six days a week and sells baking supplies ranging from food coloring and fondant to silicone molds, boxes and other decorating tools for home and professional bakers.

In her statement, Reynolds thanked the Pearl Fire Department, emergency medical personnel and the Pearl Police Department for their quick response to the fire.

“We are incredibly grateful for the work you do for our community,” she wrote.

Organizers say the fundraiser will help Reynolds replace critical equipment and continue serving local bakers as the shop works to recover.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: PEARL — A GoFundMe fundraiser has been launched to help the owner of The Edible Paper Store & Co. in Pearl recover after a fire damaged the specialty baking supply shop and forced it to close indefinitely.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Fire Damages Pearl's Edible Paper Store - Darkhorse Press

JACKSON — The Jackson Police Department is asking for the public’s help in solving a homicide that happened late last year.

According to police, the homicide happened Dec. 1, 2025, in the 200 block of Sollitt Street. The victim was identified as James Crowley Jr.

Investigators are asking anyone with information about the case to come forward.

Authorities said tips that lead to an arrest could result in a reward of up to $2,500 through Crime Stoppers.

Anyone with information about the case is asked to contact the Jackson Police Department at 601-960-1234 or Crime Stoppers at 601-355-TIPS (8477). Tips can remain anonymous.

The investigation remains ongoing.

JONES COUNTY – The Jones County Sheriff’s Department is asking for the public’s help locating a missing elderly woman.

Authorities said Nellie Rustin, 82, was reported missing after she was last seen around 6 p.m. Tuesday at her residence on Thomas Ed Rustin Road in the Rustin community.

Rustin is described as a white female, approximately 5 feet 5 inches tall and weighing about 135 pounds. She has hazel eyes and gray hair.

Deputies said anyone with information about Rustin’s whereabouts should call 911 or contact the Jones County Sheriff’s Department at 601-425-3147.

Officials did not release additional details as the search remains ongoing.

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
PEARL – The Pearl Police Department recovered about $6,500 in stolen merchandise following an investigation into a retail theft ring targeting businesses in Pearl.

Officers first responded Feb. 6 at about 1 p.m. to a reported shoplifting at Ulta Beauty. By the time officers arrived, the suspects had left with merchandise valued at about $3,150, police said.

The department’s Criminal Investigations Division opened an investigation and identified two suspects as Aureka Ducksworth and Keanna Gordon. Investigators said a third person involved had not yet been identified at that time. Officers also identified a suspect vehicle by reviewing surveillance footage.

On March 8 at about 6 p.m., officers with the Patrol Division, Special Operations Division and Criminal Investigations Division received information that the group was again targeting retail stores in Pearl. Officers later found the suspect vehicle at the Outlets of Mississippi.

A search of the vehicle led to the recovery of about $6,500 in stolen merchandise believed to be tied to multiple retail theft cases, police said.

Authorities charged Ducksworth with felony shoplifting, conspiracy and placed a hold on her for a felony shoplifting warrant from another jurisdiction.

Keanna Gordon was also charged with felony shoplifting, conspiracy and held on a felony shoplifting warrant from another jurisdiction.

Police said Sharnique Gordon, who was not pictured in the department’s release, was charged with felony shoplifting, conspiracy and held on a felony shoplifting warrant from another jurisdiction.

Investigators said they are working with multiple jurisdictions on returning the recovered merchandise and continuing the prosecution of the suspects.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: PEARL – The Pearl Police Department recovered about $6,500 in stolen merchandise following an investigation into a retail theft ring targeting businesses in Pearl.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Pearl police recover $6,500 in stolen merchandise in retail theft investigation - Darkhorse Press

WEBSTER COUNTY – A Mississippi state trooper and another driver are recovering after a crash that occurred while the trooper was pursuing another vehicle, according to WTVA.

Bryant Flake with the Mississippi Highway Patrol told WTVA that a trooper assigned to Troop G collided with another vehicle while attempting to stop a different car.

The driver of the other vehicle was transported to a hospital in Tupelo with moderate injuries. The trooper was taken to Choctaw Regional Medical Center in Ackerman, also with moderate injuries.

WTVA reported that both individuals are reported to be improving, officials said. The incident remains under investigation.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: WEBSTER COUNTY – A Mississippi state trooper and another driver are recovering after a crash that occurred while the trooper was pursuing another vehicle, according to WTVA.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: State trooper, driver injured in crash during pursuit in Webster County - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
JACKSON — Mississippi law enforcement leaders say changes to the state’s public employee retirement system have put first responders at a disadvantage and are worsening recruitment and retention challenges across the state.

Andy “Ski” Matuszewski, Mississippi division president of the Southern States Police Benevolent Association, said a task force of first responder representatives formed in July 2025 following the passage of House Bill 1 during that year’s legislative session. The measure made significant changes to the Public Employees’ Retirement System, or PERS, including the Tier 5 retirement structure for newer employees placing first responders in a 35 year plan with less benefits than under the previous retirement tier.

Matuszewski said the group, made up largely of presidents and representatives of public safety organizations, began meeting to develop a response to the legislation and advocate for Tier 5 reforms in the face of evidence of a growing disconnect between legislators and the men and women who protect and serve Mississippians.

“We felt we had no choice other than to organize a response,” Matuszewski said, noting that a study conducted by the Stennis Institute supported concerns that Mississippi’s new Tier 5 retirement benefits for first responders now lag behind neighboring states.

He said the group’s position is that public safety careers should be treated differently than other state jobs because of the physical and mental demands of the work. Higher cancer rates, heart disease, and more suicides for first responders also point to reasons why their retirement should be different, in addition to the immediate dangers of the job.

“Expecting a first responder to work for 35 years for a fraction of what the people they are working beside daily receive is not only unreasonable but patently unfair,” Matuszewski said.

Members of the task force met with lawmakers and officials several times over the past year, including a January meeting with staff from PERS and meetings with legislators supporting proposals to modify the system.

Matuszewski said the group also sought meetings with Lt. Gov. Delbert Hosemann, whose office oversees the Senate. In addition the SSPBA has supported Hosemann in the past, believing him to have first responders’ best interests in mind.

During one meeting in February at the Capitol, Matuszewski said members of the task force met with Hosemann, his staff, Sen. Daniel Sparks, who authored legislation related to Tier 5 retirement, and PERS Executive Director Ray Higgins.

According to Matuszewski and others in the meeting, the meeting began with the lieutenant governor skipping all ceremony such as handshaking and standing in front of his desk and asking the group flat out what they wanted, stating that he had to get back to the Senate floor. As the discussion continued, Sparks began explaining details of proposed legislation addressing retirement concerns.

Matuszewski and others said Hosemann interrupted Sparks during one part of the explanation and suggested the first responders present would not understand the technical details being discussed.

“He interrupted him as Sparks started to go into some detail on a particular nuance, saying words to the effect of ‘these guys won’t understand that,'” Matuszewski said.

Matuszewski said the comment was frustrating for members of the task force, which includes experienced law enforcement officers, firefighters and other public safety professionals.

The retirement debate has been a major issue during the current legislative session. On March 6, House Speaker Jason White announced that the House had amended Senate Bill 2103 by replacing its language with provisions addressing some concerns raised by first responder groups.

Matuszewski said first responders across Mississippi are closely watching the outcome.

He said the state is already struggling to recruit and retain police officers, firefighters, emergency medical personnel, corrections officers and 911 telecommunicators, and warned that failing to address future retirement benefits could push experienced workers to leave for neighboring states offering better compensation and incentives.

“If public safety doesn’t work, nothing else matters,” Matuszewski said.

The SSPBA president also noted that the first responders fighting for the Tier 5 specifications to be fair have nothing to gain from it. The whole point is future generations, not only of first responders, but of those they will be protecting.

The pushback also follows comments late last year by Lt. Gov. Delbert Hosemann, who said first responders “live longer than most people do because they stay healthy.”

Medical and occupational research has repeatedly shown that police officers and firefighters face elevated health risks that can shorten careers and lives. A long-term mortality study of police officers published in Policing: An International Journal found officers experienced a higher risk of early death compared with the general population, particularly during the first years of service.

Mental health is also a significant concern among public safety workers. Studies show that about one in four first responders experience depression, a statistic that reflects only reported and diagnosed cases.

Suicide has also emerged as a leading cause of death among first responders. The Ruderman Family Foundation reported that more police officers and firefighters died by suicide in 2017 than were killed in the line of duty, citing higher rates of depression and post-traumatic stress disorder tied to repeated exposure to traumatic incidents. Nationally, more officers die from suicide than from line-of-duty deaths in many years.

Firefighters also face increased cancer risks due to repeated exposure to hazardous materials. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention says firefighters are frequently exposed to carcinogens such as benzene, formaldehyde and asbestos during emergency responses. The agency created the National Firefighter Registry for Cancer to study those risks, while the International Association of Fire Fighters reports firefighters are more likely than the general population to develop several types of cancer.

Research also shows first responders are exposed to far more traumatic events than most civilians. The Law Enforcement Alliance for Peer Support cites research indicating first responders encounter an average of 178 critical incidents over the course of their careers, compared with two to three traumatic events experienced by the average civilian.

Occupational stress has also been linked to higher rates of family strain among public safety workers. Research published by the University of Maryland School of Public Health found that irregular schedules, cumulative trauma and shift work contribute to increased marital instability among police officers and other first responders.

Public safety organizations across Mississippi have also weighed in on the debate surrounding Senate Bill 2103 and proposed retirement changes.

In a March 6 Facebook statement, the Mississippi Association of Chiefs of Police said it appreciates the House’s efforts to work with first responders as lawmakers consider changes to the retirement system.

“We appreciate the House working with first responders and recognizing that recruitment and retention are critical for Mississippi’s public safety community,” the organization said in a Facebook statement. “First responders face extraordinary physical and mental health demands while protecting our communities, and many are looking for the stability needed to continue serving safely and effectively. We look forward to continuing to work with the Senate as discussions move forward to support those who protect Mississippi every day.”

The organization also said the House amendment maintains eight-year vesting for first responders and does not include a back-to-work provision.

The Mississippi Deputy Sheriffs Association has also urged lawmakers to continue considering the legislation, warning that allowing the bill to die could send the wrong message to public safety workers and educators across the state.

“The men and women who serve as deputies, police officers, firefighters, EMS personnel, and teachers across Mississippi do not ask for much,” the association said in a social media statement. “They simply ask for the ability to do their jobs, protect their communities, and provide for their families with dignity and security.”

The group emphasized that Senate Bill 2103 represents more than a political debate.

“SB 2103 is not a political talking point. It is about the future of public safety and education in our state,” the Mississippi Deputy Sheriff’s Association posted.

The organization also urged lawmakers to give the House amendment full consideration and called on first responders and citizens to contact senators and the lieutenant governor’s office about the issue.

“We are hearing concerning rumors that Senate leadership may allow this bill to die without proper consideration,” the Mississippi Deputy Sheriff’s Association said, encouraging first responders to attend the First Responders Caucus at the State Capitol on March 12.

“When we stand united, our voices are harder to ignore,” the association said.

Matuszewski said first responder organizations are increasingly paying attention to how lawmakers approach public safety issues and retirement policy.

He said groups representing thousands of public safety workers are closely tracking legislative votes and will likely play a larger role in evaluating candidates in future elections.

“First responders show up for the people we protect and serve each and every day,” Matuszewski said. “We have the right to expect our elected officials to do the same for us.”

An email seeking comment from Hosemann’s office was not answered by press time.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON — Mississippi law enforcement leaders say changes to the state’s public employee retirement system have put first responders at a disadvantage and are worsening recruitment and retention challenges across the state.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: 'They Won't Understand That': First Responders Say Lt. Gov. Dismissed Their Retirement Concern - Darkhorse Press

GULFPORT — Volunteers are invited to help plant trees Thursday at a Gulfport park as part of a community service event celebrating the nation’s upcoming 250th anniversary.

The City of Gulfport and the Green Infrastructure Center are hosting a Tree Planting Day at Brickyard Bayou Park, where volunteers will work to expand the city’s tree canopy and support environmental improvements.

Organizers say the event aims to promote a greener and healthier Gulfport while encouraging community members to participate in service projects tied to the nation’s 250th anniversary.

Volunteers will meet at the park’s trailhead at 3348 Johnson Drive. Registration begins at 8:30 a.m., followed by a brief training at 9 a.m. Tree planting is scheduled from 9:30 a.m. to 4 p.m.

Organizers say the project will help enhance green spaces in the area and benefit the community for years to come.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: GULFPORT — Volunteers are invited to help plant trees Thursday at a Gulfport park as part of a community service event celebrating the nation’s upcoming 250th anniversary.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Volunteers invited to Gulfport tree-planting event - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
YAZOO COUNTY — A woman was taken into custody early Monday after deputies say she fled from law enforcement and struck two patrol vehicles on the grounds of a correctional facility in Yazoo County.

The incident began around 3 a.m. when deputies encountered a white vehicle whose driver had reportedly called a tow truck driver in Madison County to change a tire. After the tow truck driver installed the tire, the driver of the vehicle allegedly left the scene without paying for the service.

The tow truck driver later contacted Yazoo County dispatch after the vehicle crossed into Yazoo County, authorities said.

Deputies quickly located the vehicle and attempted a traffic stop, but the driver fled, leading deputies on a pursuit.

During the chase, the driver turned onto the grounds of a correctional facility in Yazoo County, where authorities say she struck two patrol vehicles while continuing to try to evade deputies.

The collisions eventually disabled the suspect’s vehicle, and deputies took the driver into custody at the scene.

Authorities said neither of the deputies involved was injured, and the driver was also not injured.

Additional details, including the driver’s identity and potential charges, were not immediately released.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: YAZOO COUNTY — A woman was taken into custody early Monday after deputies say she fled from law enforcement and struck two patrol vehicles on the grounds of a correctional facility in Yazoo County.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Woman Arrested After Pursuit, Collisions With Patrol Vehicles in Yazoo County - Darkhorse Press

Authorities said Deputy Wilkins noticed a vehicle traveling northbound without a tag and initiated a traffic stop.

Two men were inside the vehicle, and both were taken into custody without incident.

No additional information was immediately released by the sheriff’s department.

The Covington County Sheriff’s Department said it is seeking information that could help identify the individuals. Officials released the image of the two men on Facebook and are asking anyone who recognizes them to come forward.

Officials thanked the public for its continued support in helping law enforcement serve and protect Covington County.

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
JONES COUNTY – The Jones County Sheriff’s Department is warning residents about a scam in which a caller is impersonating a sheriff’s deputy and threatening arrest over missed jury duty.

Authorities said the scammer contacts potential victims by phone and claims they must pay a $3,000 fine or face arrest for failing to report for jury duty.

Investigators said the caller instructs victims not to tell anyone about the payment and directs them to purchase a debit-style gift card and provide the card number over the phone or send money through a cash transfer application.

Officials said these types of payments cannot be recovered once sent, which allows the scammer to keep the money.

The sheriff’s department said residents should not provide payment or personal information to anyone making these claims and should block the caller’s phone number if possible.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JONES COUNTY – The Jones County Sheriff’s Department is warning residents about a scam in which a caller is impersonating a sheriff’s deputy and threatening arrest over missed jury duty.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Jones County officials warn residents about jury duty scam - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
BILOXI — Authorities say a Texas man was arrested after nearly nine pounds of cocaine were discovered during a traffic stop on Interstate 10 in Harrison County.

Matt Haley announced Thursday that deputies with the Harrison County Sheriff’s Office seized 8.8 pounds of cocaine during a stop along Interstate 10 eastbound near the 40-mile marker in Biloxi.

According to the sheriff’s office, a deputy assigned to the Criminal Interdiction Division and an agent with the U.S. Border Patrol conducted the traffic stop at about 11:08 a.m. March 11 on a black Jeep Wagoneer for a traffic violation.

During the stop, the driver identified himself as Norbert Henry Jr. of Houston, Texas. A search of the vehicle led deputies to discover 8.8 pounds of cocaine hidden inside the vehicle, authorities said.

Investigators with the Harrison County Sheriff’s Office narcotics division responded to the scene and took over the case.

Henry was booked into the Harrison County jail and charged with trafficking cocaine. Authorities did not release additional details about the investigation.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: BILOXI — Authorities say a Texas man was arrested after nearly nine pounds of cocaine were discovered during a traffic stop on Interstate 10 in Harrison County.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Man arrested after 8.8 pounds of cocaine seized on I-10 - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Cash Matlock

MERIDIAN — The Meridian Police Department is investigating a shooting after officers initially responded to a reported motor vehicle accident Wednesday evening.

Police said 911 received a call around 7:15 p.m. about a crash in the area of 18th Street between 30th and 31st avenues. Officers were dispatched to the scene and worked to ensure the victim received medical attention.

Authorities later determined the individual involved had suffered a gunshot wound, according to the Meridian Police Department.

The victim was transported to a local hospital before being airlifted to a hospital in Jackson for further treatment. The person’s condition has not been released.

Police said the case remains an active and ongoing investigation, and limited details were immediately available as detectives continue gathering information.

Officials said additional details will be released as they become available.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: MERIDIAN — The Meridian Police Department is investigating a shooting after officers initially responded to a reported motor vehicle accident Wednesday evening.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Meridian police investigating shooting after reported vehicle accident - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
STATEWIDE — Authorities have released new images of a Tennessee’s Most Wanted suspect accused of shooting a Memphis police officer as the search for him expands across several states, including into Louisiana.

The Tennessee Bureau of Investigation said the search continues for Danell Maxwell, who is wanted in connection with the Feb. 26 shooting of Oscar Torres-Molina during a traffic stop in Memphis.

Investigators say Maxwell allegedly shot Torres-Molina in the face during the encounter. The officer survived the shooting.

Maxwell is wanted on charges of attempted first-degree murder, aggravated assault and employment of a firearm during the commission of a dangerous felony, according to the TBI.

Authorities released new photos of Maxwell, including an AI-generated image showing what he may look like without facial hair, along with an earlier photo showing him with a beard. Investigators said the images are intended to help the public recognize him if his appearance has changed since the shooting.

Officials believe Maxwell may have left Tennessee and could be in Arkansas, Louisiana, Georgia or Iowa. Law enforcement is asking residents in those states to remain alert.

Maxwell is described as 5 feet 10 inches tall, about 180 pounds, with black hair and brown eyes, and authorities say he should be considered armed and dangerous.

The TBI is offering a reward of up to $32,500 for information leading to his arrest.

Anyone who sees Maxwell or has information about his whereabouts is asked to contact the TBI at 1-800-TBI-FIND.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: STATEWIDE — Authorities have released new images of a Tennessee’s Most Wanted suspect accused of shooting a Memphis police officer as the search for him expands across several states, including into Louisiana.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: New images released of Tennessee cop-shooting suspect as search expands - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
PEARL – State officials are assessing damage across several Mississippi counties after severe weather moved through the state Wednesday, leaving one person dead and thousands without electricity.

According to the Mississippi Emergency Management Agency, the death and three injuries were reported in Lawrence County following the storms on March 11.

As of 9:30 a.m. Thursday, about 40,300 power outages were reported statewide. Officials said outages had peaked at more than 48,000 during the height of the storms.

Preliminary reports show storm damage in six counties: Amite, Lawrence, Lincoln, Smith, Pike and Walthall. Officials said damage reports include homes damaged, downed trees and widespread power outages.

MEMA said the numbers are preliminary and could change as damage assessments continue.

Residents who experienced storm damage are encouraged to file insurance claims, take photos of the damage and report it to their county emergency management agency or through MEMA’s online self-reporting tool.

Officials also urged residents with immediate unmet needs to contact their local county emergency management agency for assistance.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: PEARL – State officials are assessing damage across several Mississippi counties after severe weather moved through the state Wednesday, leaving one person dead and thousands without electricity.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Severe storms leave one dead, thousands without power across Mississippi - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
STARKVILLE — City officials are encouraging residents to mow less this spring as part of a strategy aimed at supporting wildflower growth and helping pollinators thrive.

The City of Starkville announced it will implement a reduced mowing approach in select areas this season, allowing naturally occurring wildflowers to grow and provide food for bees, butterflies, birds and other wildlife emerging after winter.

Officials say the effort also beautifies the community while reducing costs associated with fuel, herbicides and routine mowing.

“We’re fortunate in Mississippi to have so many wildflowers that appear this time of year,” said Chris Smiley, director of Sanitation and Environmental Services. “They’re an important part of Starkville’s natural beauty and a key indicator of a healthy environment.”

A variety of spring wildflowers commonly appear in local lawns and green spaces, including spring beauty, daisy fleabane, ranunculus, oxalis, jonquil, phlox, violets and clover. Many of these plants act as “companion plants,” which grow alongside turfgrass and can help improve soil health, reduce pests and suppress weeds.

Experts say protecting wildflowers can play an important role in supporting pollinators, which have experienced significant population declines in recent decades due to habitat loss, pesticide use and invasive species. Research suggests about 40% of invertebrate pollinators could face extinction in the coming decades.

Bob Brzuszek, professor emeritus in the Department of Landscape Architecture at Mississippi State University, said even small changes in mowing habits can help.

“It’s not a lack of care if you don’t mow — you’re actually caring about pollinators,” Brzuszek said, suggesting homeowners delay mowing for a week or two to see what wildflowers naturally emerge.

Some homeowners, he said, choose to mow around patches of flowers, creating colorful “islands” in the grass while preserving habitat for wildlife. Others plant native wildflower seeds to encourage biodiversity and beautify their yards.

City officials say the reduced mowing strategy highlights the natural beauty of the Starkville area while supporting a healthier local ecosystem.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: STARKVILLE — City officials are encouraging residents to mow less this spring as part of a strategy aimed at supporting wildflower growth and helping pollinators thrive.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Starkville encourages reduced mowing to support wildflowers - Darkhorse Press

ADAMS COUNTY – Deputies with the Adams County Sheriff’s Office responded early Thursday morning to a single-vehicle crash after a car struck a fallen tree blocking the roadway.

The crash occurred around 5:45 a.m. March 12 in the 700 block of Old Highway 84 near 779 Old Highway 84 #3, according to the sheriff’s office.

Authorities said a 911 caller reported that a vehicle had collided with a tree that had fallen across the road. When deputies arrived, they found a black Nissan sedan that had struck the tree, which was blocking the roadway.

Officials said the impact caused significant damage to the front of the vehicle.

Deputies secured the scene while the Adams County Road Department responded to remove the fallen tree. The roadway has since been cleared and reopened to normal traffic.

Officials said no additional information about injuries was available at the time of the report.

The Adams County Sheriff’s Office reminded motorists to use caution when traveling, especially during overnight and early morning hours when fallen limbs or trees may be present following storms or high winds.

By William H. Kelly III Jackson State University

JACKSON – Ten Jackson State University students are recipients of a $75,000 scholarship from New York Times best-selling author James Patterson and the Patterson Family Foundation. The scholarship awards $7,500 each to five undergraduate and graduate students pursuing a career in writing.

“This transformative gift from the Patterson Family Foundation is one of the most significant contributions to support writing education in Jackson State’s history,” said Sloan Cargill, vice president for institutional advancement and external affairs and executive director of the JSU Development Foundation. “Through this investment, we are building upon our strong tradition of literary creation and education in support of future writers and writing instructors.”

In addition to exemplifying academic excellence, students submitted samples of their literary work to a scholarship committee and are pursuing a degree or minor in creative writing. The creative writing program was established in 2021 in the College of Liberal Arts.

Grace Ann Elinsky attained her undergraduate degree in creative writing from JSU and is currently a graduate student and research assistant in the Department of English and Modern Languages in the College of Liberal Arts. Elinsky says the opportunity allows her to focus more on her craft as a writer and to pour into other rising artists through outlets such as the Jackson State Writers Association, which she founded.

“I always wanted to be, in the long run, a professor of literature, fiction or creative writing, and then, a writer. As an artist, funding really helps open up space to be more creative and have fewer responsibilities, such as working several jobs,” she said. “In the long run, this scholarship will allow equipped and healthy artists in the world to then pass the baton and create more healthy artists.”

Ladorian Shelton, a member of the JSWA and Southern Labor Youth Movement at JSU, was emotional after learning that he was awarded the scholarship, noting that now his “entire balance is covered” for the academic year.

“I’m very blessed to be in this position. When I was told that I would be able to go to school without having to worry about working full-time like I was, I honestly cried,” said Shelton. “I’ve felt like I truly belong here at JSU. I’ve been able to make my mark on the world. I’m so grateful–so, so, so grateful that I got that acceptance letter from JSU, and I’ve gotten to be a Tiger. I believe that this has been some of the greatest times of my life.”

Shelton shared that many of his works are fiction and that he enjoys slam poetry, scriptwriting and drawing. He aspires to become a professional artist and says that his joy for writing is “pure.”

“I’ve always had one thing, and that is my love for writing. I want to make art, and that’s just the purest form of my goal. When I graduate, I would like to get paid for creating, but if I don’t, then I will still be creating. It’s not about the money for me. It’s not about fame. It’s just the pure joy that I get in writing.”

Ebony Lumumba, Ph.D., chair of the EML department, says the Patterson Family Foundation scholarship not only demonstrates that students are interested in writing and the arts, but that there is financial support available for them.

“This is such a substantial award, specifically to support the arts, isolated for students to be creative. Being creative is a courageous feat, so to have their courage affirmed and acknowledged in a way that’s tangible, that will get them to the next step, really makes all the difference for what you can be bold about doing with your future and with your talent,” said Lumumba.

The list of JSU awardees includes:
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: By William H. Kelly III Jackson State University
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Best-selling author James Patterson awards $75,000 scholarship for ten JSU students - Darkhorse Press

MARKS – Mississippi Attorney General Lynn Fitch announced the sentencing of a former city clerk who admitted to embezzling nearly $150,000 from the City of Marks.

According to the Attorney General’s Office, Pamela Latrice McNutt, 44, was convicted of one count of embezzlement by a public official. Investigators said McNutt stole the funds while serving as the city clerk.

The case was investigated and prosecuted by the Mississippi Attorney General’s Office after a referral from the Office of the State Auditor.

“Our office will continue to hold those who defraud our communities accountable,” Fitch said. “This abuse was hurtful, and it affected hardworking Mississippians. I am grateful to our incredible team of investigators and prosecutors who ensured that justice was served in this case.”

On March 2, 2026, Eleventh Circuit Court Judge Linda Coleman sentenced McNutt to 12 years in the custody of the Mississippi Department of Corrections.

Officials said the sentence includes three years to be served on the Intensive Supervision Program, with nine years suspended. McNutt will also serve five years of supervised probation followed by four years of unsupervised probation.

In addition, the court ordered McNutt to pay $1,000 in fines and a $500 assessment to the Crime Victim Compensation Fund. She also forfeited her $50,000 security bond and must pay $98,428.68 in restitution.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: MARKS – Mississippi Attorney General Lynn Fitch announced the sentencing of a former city clerk who admitted to embezzling nearly $150,000 from the City of Marks.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Former Marks city clerk sentenced in embezzlement case - Darkhorse Press

JACKSON – Airmen and Soldiers from several Mississippi National Guard units deployed in late February to the U.S. Central Command area of responsibility in support of Operation Epic Fury, according to the Mississippi National Guard.

Units participating in the deployment include the 186th Air Refueling Wing, the 172d Airlift Wing, the 2-20th Special Forces Battalion and the Special Operations Detachment.

Mississippi Gov. Tate Reeves praised the service members and their families as they begin the mission.

“Mississippi is praying for the brave members of the Mississippi National Guard and all U.S. service members who have been deployed as part of Operation Epic Fury,” Reeves said. “Our state’s guardsmen are truly the best of the best, and Mississippi will always be grateful to them and their families for courageously defending our nation.”

Maj. Gen. Bobby M. Ginn Jr., the Adjutant General of Mississippi, said the deployment reflects the readiness and commitment of the state’s military personnel.

“Mississippi’s Airmen and Soldiers stand ready to serve wherever they are needed,” Ginn said. “Supporting this operation highlights the training, commitment and capability that define the Mississippi National Guard.”

Officials said the deployment brings together multiple Mississippi National Guard units working as a joint force to support operations overseas.

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
RANKIN COUNTY, Miss. — The Rankin County Prosecuting Attorney’s Office is warning drivers that operating a vehicle while under the influence of marijuana remains illegal under Mississippi law.

Prosecuting Attorney Trey Spillman said his office has seen an increase in DUI cases involving marijuana and wants the public to understand how the law applies, particularly since the launch of Mississippi’s medical cannabis program.

According to Mississippi law, it is unlawful for a person to operate a motor vehicle while under the influence of any drug or controlled substance, including marijuana.

Spillman said many people mistakenly believe DUI laws apply only to alcohol or that there must be a specific THC level similar to the .08 blood alcohol concentration standard used in alcohol-related DUI cases.

“That is not how the law works,” Spillman said.

Under Mississippi law, a person is considered under the influence if a substance affects their ability to safely operate a vehicle by lessening normal clarity, judgment or control.

In marijuana-related DUI cases, impairment is often determined based on the totality of the circumstances. Evidence may include driving behavior observed by law enforcement, physical indicators such as bloodshot eyes or slowed reactions, statements made by the driver, field sobriety tests, the odor or presence of marijuana and toxicology testing when available.

Spillman noted that toxicology tests can confirm exposure to marijuana but do not always determine when the substance was consumed or whether the driver was impaired at the time of driving. Because of that, officer observations and other evidence can play a significant role in these cases.

The Mississippi Medical Cannabis Act also does not permit driving while impaired by marijuana, even if the substance was legally obtained for medical use.

Spillman said the same principle applies to prescription medications that may affect a driver’s ability to safely operate a vehicle.

Officials said driving under the influence of any substance places others on the road at risk, and prosecutors remain committed to enforcing Mississippi’s DUI laws to protect public safety.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: RANKIN COUNTY, Miss. — The Rankin County Prosecuting Attorney’s Office is warning drivers that operating a vehicle while under the influence of marijuana remains illegal under Mississippi law.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Rankin County prosecutor warns drivers about marijuana-related DUIs - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
STATEWIDE — Americans are celebrating National K-9 Veterans Day on March 13, recognizing the service and sacrifice of military and working dogs that have served alongside humans in combat, law enforcement and rescue operations.

The day commemorates March 13, 1942, when the United States Army officially began training dogs for military use through the K-9 Corps, marking the first time dogs were formally incorporated into the U.S. armed forces.

The program was launched during World War II after the attack on Pearl Harbor prompted a group of civilians to form Dogs for Defense, a coalition that helped recruit and train dogs for sentry duty and other wartime missions.

Early in the program, American families donated many of the dogs that would become the first generation of military working dogs. Within a year, more than 1,800 trained dog teams were patrolling U.S. coastlines and supporting military operations.

Over time, the role of military working dogs expanded far beyond sentry duty. Dogs have served as scouts, messengers and mine-detection specialists, and they continue to assist with missions ranging from explosives detection to tracking and search-and-rescue operations.

Several military dogs have become famous for their service, including Sgt. Stubby, one of the earliest and most celebrated war dogs; Chips, considered the most decorated dog of World War II; Lex, who was adopted by the family of his fallen handler; and Cairo, who participated in the 2011 mission that killed Osama bin Laden.

Today, military working dogs serve in all branches of the armed forces and assist agencies including the U.S. Coast Guard, Federal Bureau of Investigation and United States Secret Service, as well as police departments and border security units across the country.

Many of the dogs now receive retirement ceremonies, medals and memorial services recognizing their service. Observers say the annual holiday is a chance to reflect on the thousands of canine partners that have saved lives, protected troops and helped keep communities safe.

Each year on March 13, supporters pause to honor those four-legged veterans who have served with loyalty, courage and an unwavering commitment to their human partners.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: STATEWIDE — Americans are celebrating National K-9 Veterans Day on March 13, recognizing the service and sacrifice of military and working dogs that have served alongside humans in combat, law enforcement and rescue operations.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Today is K-9 Veterans Day, honoring working dogs - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard

GRENADA COUNTY – Two people were taken into custody Thursday in Grenada County after deputies stopped a vehicle believed to be connected to a multistate cybercrime investigation, according to the Grenada County Sheriff’s Office.

Authorities said the stop happened March 12 at about 12:30 p.m. after investigators received information that a white Toyota Highlander with Virginia license plates was traveling north on Interstate 55 near the Holmes County and Carroll County line approaching Grenada County.

The Grenada County Sheriff’s Office said the information came through a joint effort involving the Jefferson Parish Sheriff’s Office Economic Crimes Unit in Louisiana and the Mississippi Attorney General’s Office Cyber Crime Division-Cyber Fraud Task Force.

Investigators said the occupants of the vehicle were wanted in connection with cybercrimes allegedly committed in Texas and Louisiana, with other states possibly involved as the investigation continues.

Deputies stopped the vehicle and arrested Cosmin Vaduva and Elena Caldareasa.

During a search of the vehicle, authorities said deputies recovered numerous documents, thousands of dollars in U.S. currency, multiple credit, debit and gift cards, various financial instruments and three overlay skimmers.

Officials said the items were seized in connection with the suspects’ alleged crimes.

Vaduva and Caldareasa are being held in the Grenada County Jail on charges in Grenada County and are awaiting extradition to Louisiana.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: GRENADA COUNTY – Two people were taken into custody Thursday in Grenada County after deputies stopped a vehicle believed to be connected to a multistate cybercrime investigation, according to the Grenada County Sheriff’s Office.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Two arrested in Grenada County traffic stop tied to multistate cybercrime investigation - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Special to Darkhorse Press
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
HATTIESBURG — The University of Southern Mississippi celebrated the formal opening of the Paul H. “Bud” Holmes Criminal Justice Building, a newly renovated home for the School of Criminal Justice, Forensic Science and Security with a ribbon-cutting ceremony on Friday, March 13.

This state-of-the-art facility, which has undergone a $16.6 million renovation, is the result of generous support from alumnus Paul H. “Bud” Holmes and strategic investments by the State of Mississippi and the University.

Adjacent to M.M. Roberts Stadium, the Holmes Criminal Justice Building will provide students with modern spaces designed for hands-on learning, collaboration and real-life experiences, including specialized laboratories and an academic courtroom, that will shape the trajectory of the program for years to come.

“Today is a day of pride, gratitude and promise for our University and for the future of the School of Criminal Justice, Forensic Science and Security. The pursuit of justice is a profound calling, one that demands courage, integrity and an unwavering commitment to serving others,” said Dr. Joseph Paul, president of The University of Southern Mississippi. “This facility ensures they will graduate ready to enter courtrooms, laboratories and agencies with confidence — prepared to shoulder responsibility, to seek truth and to serve with honor.”

Influenced by his father, D.W. Holmes, a prosecutor, municipal judge and former mayor of Hattiesburg, Paul H. “Bud” Holmes has built a life defined by service, leadership and a commitment to justice. His career has spanned military service, the legal profession, public leadership and professional athletics, experiences that shaped his belief in accountability and public responsibility.

A lifelong Pine Belt resident and dedicated supporter of The University of Southern Mississippi, Holmes’ recent philanthropic investment supports the completion and furnishing of the Paul H. “Bud” Holmes Criminal Justice Building while establishing an endowment to provide lasting resources for the School and the students it serves.

“No matter how much I give, I will never be able to repay all that Southern Miss has given me,” said Holmes. “When my children walk by, they can point with pride to this building and know their dad gave back to the University that gave so much to him.”

The renovated facility is designed to align with real-world career preparation. With new classroom spaces, collaborative study areas, advanced forensic science labs and a mock courtroom, students will have unprecedented opportunities that mirror on-the-job training.

“The experiential learning opportunities housed within these walls will strengthen recruitment, enhance research and partnerships and ensure our graduates leave Southern Miss exceptionally prepared to lead and serve in their fields,” said Dr. Chris Winstead, dean of the College of Arts and Sciences. “Mr. Holmes’ support reflects a deep belief in our students and in the vital work they are called to do.”

The ribbon-cutting ceremony marks the beginning of a new era for the School of Criminal Justice, Forensic Science and Security, one rooted in innovation, community engagement and industry-aligned training. More importantly, it stands as a powerful reminder for how private philanthropy can accelerate the University’s mission and expand what is possible for its students.

“We are profoundly grateful for the generous support that made this facility possible,” said Stace Mercier, executive director of the USM Foundation. “Mr. Holmes has been a dedicated supporter and advocate of our institution for many years, and this investment reflects his enduring belief in our students and in the vital work they are preparing to do. His generosity will impact generations of justice seekers who begin their journeys here at Southern Miss.”

Learn more about the Paul H. “Bud” Holmes Criminal Justice Building and the School of Criminal Justice, Forensic Science and Security at The University of Southern Mississippi. Visit the USM Foundation for information about supporting the program and other student-centered initiatives.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: HATTIESBURG — The University of Southern Mississippi celebrated the formal opening of the Paul H. “Bud” Holmes Criminal Justice Building, a newly renovated home for the School of Criminal Justice, Forensic Science and Security with a ribbon-cutting ceremony on Friday, March 13.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: USM Celebrates Ribbon Cutting for the Paul H. “Bud” Holmes Criminal Justice Building - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/top-s.content_html:
STATEWIDE — Mississippi residents are among the most likely in the country to feel stuck in repetitive daily routines that lead to boredom and increased screen time, according to a new national surve y.

The study by Solitaired ranked Mississippi No. 4 in the United States for “boredom loops,” a term researchers used to describe cycles where daily routines feel repetitive and mentally draining.

Researchers surveyed thousands of Americans about how often they feel bored, how repetitive their routines are and how those feelings affect motivation and technology use.

Among Mississippi respondents, 61% said their lives feel repetitive, the third-highest rate in the nation. The survey also found that 65% said repetitive routines are mentally exhausting, while 61% reported feeling less motivated at work when their days feel the same.

Screen use appears closely tied to those feelings. The study found 74% of Mississippi residents scroll on their phones multiple times a day to escape boredom — the highest percentage in the country. Another 84% said they spend more time scrolling or watching television when their days feel repetitive, also ranking third nationally.

Researchers also found 67% of respondents said they tend to procrastinate or disengage when their days feel repetitive, and 35% reported losing track of time once they start scrolling on their phones and feeling worse afterward.

The survey was conducted to better understand how boredom affects everyday routines across the United States. Researchers asked residents in each state about their daily habits, how often they feel bored and what activities they turn to when life feels monotonous.

Responses reflecting boredom-related behaviors were scored on a 0–100 scale, with higher scores indicating stronger patterns of repetitive routines and boredom.

The findings suggest that routine fatigue and digital habits may be reinforcing a cycle for many Americans, particularly in states where residents report feeling stuck in repetitive daily patterns.
